description: FEDRAMP (Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program) is a US government-wide program that provides a standardized approach to security assessment, authorization, and continuous monitoring for cloud products and services. This domain knowledge provides role-based guidance and use cases for processing FEDRAMP compliance-related data questions.
detailed_domain_knowledge: FEDRAMP (Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program) is a US government-wide program that standardizes security assessment, authorization, and continuous monitoring for cloud products and services used by federal agencies. FEDRAMP was established to accelerate the adoption of secure cloud services by providing a standardized approach to security assessment and authorization. The program is managed by the General Services Administration (GSA) in partnership with the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and the Department of Defense (DOD). FEDRAMP uses the NIST Special Publication 800-53 security controls as the baseline, with three impact levels: Low Impact (LI), Moderate Impact (MI), and High Impact (HI). Key components include: Security Assessment (3PAO assessment, security control testing, vulnerability scanning, penetration testing), Authorization Package (System Security Plan, Security Assessment Report, Plan of Action and Milestones, Continuous Monitoring Strategy), Authorization Process (Agency authorization, Joint Authorization Board authorization, Provisional Authorization to Operate), Continuous Monitoring (ongoing security monitoring, annual assessments, incident reporting, change management). FEDRAMP authorization types include: Agency Authorization (single agency sponsors and authorizes), JAB Authorization (Joint Authorization Board authorizes for government-wide use), and P-ATO (Provisional Authorization to Operate for high-impact systems). Key concepts include: 3PAO (Third Party Assessment Organization), SSP (System Security Plan), SAR (Security Assessment Report), POA&M (Plan of Action and Milestones), ATO (Authorization to Operate), P-ATO (Provisional ATO), continuous monitoring, security control inheritance, and control baselines. FEDRAMP compliance requires comprehensive security controls, rigorous assessment, ongoing monitoring, and regular re-authorization.

roles.description: Responsible for overall FEDRAMP compliance program, managing security controls, and coordinating authorization activities.
roles.detailed_knowledge: The FEDRAMP Security Officer is responsible for the overall FEDRAMP compliance program and authorization activities. This role requires deep understanding of FEDRAMP requirements, NIST 800-53 controls, security assessment processes, and authorization procedures. Key activities include developing and maintaining the System Security Plan (SSP), coordinating security control implementation, managing relationships with 3PAO (Third Party Assessment Organization), coordinating security assessments, developing and maintaining POA&M (Plan of Action and Milestones), managing continuous monitoring activities, coordinating with authorizing officials, preparing authorization packages, managing re-authorization activities, and reporting security status. The Security Officer must understand FEDRAMP authorization types (Agency Authorization, JAB Authorization, P-ATO), impact levels (Low, Moderate, High), security control baselines, control inheritance, continuous monitoring requirements, and re-authorization processes. They work with technical teams to implement controls, with 3PAO for assessments, with authorizing officials for authorization, and with federal agencies as customers. The role requires strong project management, security expertise, and communication skills. Evidence of effective performance includes comprehensive SSP, successful security assessments, maintained POA&M, effective continuous monitoring, and successful authorization.
responsibilities: Develop and maintain System Security Plan
key_questions: What is required for FEDRAMP authorization?
What security controls must be implemented?
How do we prepare for 3PAO assessment?
What is included in the authorization package?
What are continuous monitoring requirements?

roles.description: Implements and maintains NIST 800-53 security controls required for FEDRAMP compliance.
roles.detailed_knowledge: The Security Control Implementer is responsible for implementing and maintaining NIST 800-53 security controls required for FEDRAMP compliance. This role requires technical expertise in information security, cloud security, and NIST 800-53 control requirements. Key activities include understanding control requirements from NIST 800-53, implementing controls according to control baselines (Low, Moderate, High impact), documenting control implementation in the SSP, testing control effectiveness, maintaining controls, updating control documentation, addressing control deficiencies, and supporting security assessments. The implementer must understand NIST 800-53 control families (Access Control, Audit and Accountability, Security Assessment, Configuration Management, etc.), control baselines for different impact levels, control implementation approaches, control testing requirements, and control documentation requirements. They work with the FEDRAMP Security Officer to understand requirements, with technical teams to implement controls, and with 3PAO during assessments. The role requires strong technical skills, understanding of security controls, and attention to detail. Evidence of effective implementation includes implemented controls, control documentation, test results, and positive assessment findings.
key_questions: What NIST 800-53 controls must be implemented?
What is the control baseline for our impact level?
How do we implement this control?
How do we test control effectiveness?
What documentation is needed for controls?

roles.description: Coordinates with Third Party Assessment Organizations (3PAO) for security assessments and maintains assessment relationships.
roles.detailed_knowledge: The 3PAO Liaison coordinates with Third Party Assessment Organizations (3PAO) that conduct independent security assessments required for FEDRAMP authorization. 3PAOs are accredited organizations that perform security assessments of cloud service offerings. Key activities include selecting and engaging 3PAO, coordinating assessment schedules, providing access to systems and documentation, responding to 3PAO requests, reviewing assessment findings, addressing assessment findings, coordinating remediation activities, and maintaining 3PAO relationships. The liaison must understand 3PAO requirements, assessment processes, assessment scope, assessment deliverables (Security Assessment Report), and how to work effectively with 3PAOs. They work with the FEDRAMP Security Officer, technical teams, and 3PAO assessors. The role requires project management skills, understanding of security assessments, and relationship management. Evidence of effective coordination includes successful assessments, comprehensive Security Assessment Reports, addressed findings, and positive 3PAO relationships.
responsibilities: Provide access to systems and documentation
key_questions: How do we select a 3PAO?
What is the 3PAO assessment process?
What documentation do 3PAOs need?
How do we address assessment findings?
What is included in the Security Assessment Report?

roles.description: Manages continuous monitoring activities required for FEDRAMP compliance and ongoing authorization.
roles.detailed_knowledge: The Continuous Monitoring Manager manages ongoing security monitoring activities required for FEDRAMP compliance. Continuous monitoring is essential for maintaining authorization and demonstrating ongoing security. Key activities include developing continuous monitoring strategy, implementing monitoring tools and processes, collecting security metrics and data, analyzing security data, identifying security issues, reporting security status, managing POA&M items, coordinating annual assessments, and maintaining continuous monitoring documentation. The manager must understand FEDRAMP continuous monitoring requirements, security metrics, monitoring tools, incident response, POA&M management, and reporting requirements. They work with security teams, operations teams, and the FEDRAMP Security Officer. The role requires understanding of security monitoring, metrics, and ongoing compliance. Evidence of effective continuous monitoring includes monitoring strategies, security metrics reports, incident reports, POA&M management, and annual assessment results.
key_questions: What are FEDRAMP continuous monitoring requirements?
What security metrics should we monitor?
How do we implement continuous monitoring?
How do we manage POA&M items?
What is required for annual assessments?

roles.description: Coordinates development and submission of FEDRAMP authorization packages to authorizing officials.
roles.detailed_knowledge: The Authorization Package Coordinator coordinates development and submission of FEDRAMP authorization packages. Authorization packages are comprehensive documents submitted to authorizing officials for authorization decisions. Key activities include understanding authorization package requirements, coordinating package development (SSP, SAR, POA&M, Continuous Monitoring Strategy), ensuring package completeness, reviewing package quality, coordinating package submission, responding to authorizing official questions, managing authorization process, and maintaining authorization records. The coordinator must understand FEDRAMP authorization package requirements, authorization types (Agency, JAB, P-ATO), authorization process, authorizing official expectations, and package documentation standards. They work with the FEDRAMP Security Officer, technical teams, 3PAO, and authorizing officials. The role requires project management, documentation, and communication skills. Evidence of effective coordination includes comprehensive authorization packages, successful submissions, positive authorizing official feedback, and successful authorizations.
key_questions: What is included in a FEDRAMP authorization package?
How do we develop the authorization package?
What are authorizing official requirements?
What is the authorization process?
How do we maintain authorization?

use_cases.name: FEDRAMP Authorization Process
use_cases.description: Manages the FEDRAMP authorization process from initial assessment through authorization and ongoing monitoring.
use_cases.detailed_knowledge: The FEDRAMP Authorization Process is a comprehensive process for achieving and maintaining FEDRAMP authorization. Key phases include: Preparation Phase (determining impact level, selecting authorization type, engaging 3PAO, developing SSP), Assessment Phase (3PAO security assessment, control testing, vulnerability scanning, penetration testing, Security Assessment Report), Authorization Package Development (completing SSP, compiling SAR, developing POA&M, creating Continuous Monitoring Strategy), Authorization Submission (submitting package to authorizing official, responding to questions, addressing concerns), Authorization Decision (receiving ATO, P-ATO, or denial), Continuous Monitoring (ongoing security monitoring, annual assessments, incident reporting, POA&M management), and Re-authorization (periodic re-authorization, typically every 3 years). Authorization types include: Agency Authorization (single federal agency sponsors and authorizes), JAB Authorization (Joint Authorization Board authorizes for government-wide use), and P-ATO (Provisional Authorization to Operate for high-impact systems). The process requires comprehensive security controls, rigorous assessment, detailed documentation, and ongoing monitoring. Effective authorization process management ensures successful authorization and ongoing compliance.
use_cases.business_value: Enables cloud service providers to achieve FEDRAMP authorization, allowing them to serve federal agencies and demonstrate security compliance.
example_queries: What is the FEDRAMP authorization process?
What is required for JAB authorization?
How do we prepare for authorization?
What is the timeline for authorization?

use_cases.name: NIST 800-53 Control Implementation
use_cases.description: Implements and maintains NIST 800-53 security controls required for FEDRAMP compliance based on impact level.
use_cases.detailed_knowledge: NIST 800-53 Control Implementation involves implementing security controls from NIST Special Publication 800-53 based on the system's impact level (Low, Moderate, or High). NIST 800-53 provides security controls organized into control families: AC (Access Control), AU (Audit and Accountability), CA (Security Assessment and Authorization), CM (Configuration Management), CP (Contingency Planning), IA (Identification and Authentication), IR (Incident Response), MA (Maintenance), MP (Media Protection), PE (Physical and Environmental Protection), PL (Planning), PS (Personnel Security), RA (Risk Assessment), SA (System and Services Acquisition), SC (System and Communications Protection), SI (System and Information Integrity), SR (Supply Chain Risk Management). Control baselines define which controls are required for each impact level. Low Impact baseline has fewer controls, Moderate Impact has more controls, and High Impact has the most comprehensive controls. Control implementation requires: understanding control requirements, implementing controls according to baseline, documenting implementation in SSP, testing control effectiveness, maintaining controls, and addressing deficiencies. Controls can be implemented by the cloud service provider, inherited from underlying infrastructure, or provided by third parties. Effective control implementation ensures security and FEDRAMP compliance.
use_cases.business_value: Implements comprehensive security controls to protect federal data and achieve FEDRAMP compliance, enabling cloud services for federal agencies.
example_queries: What NIST 800-53 controls must be implemented?
What is the control baseline for Moderate Impact?
How do we implement access control requirements?
How do we document control implementation?

use_cases.name: System Security Plan Development
use_cases.description: Develops and maintains the System Security Plan (SSP) required for FEDRAMP authorization.
use_cases.detailed_knowledge: System Security Plan (SSP) Development is critical for FEDRAMP authorization. The SSP is a comprehensive document that describes the system, security controls, and how controls are implemented. Key sections include: System Identification (system name, description, boundaries, categorization), System Categorization (impact levels for confidentiality, integrity, availability), System Owner Information, System Architecture (system components, data flows, network architecture), Security Control Implementation (for each control: implementation approach, responsible party, implementation details, inherited controls), Control Inheritance (controls inherited from underlying infrastructure or third parties), Common Controls (controls implemented at organizational level), System-Specific Controls (controls implemented specifically for this system), and Continuous Monitoring Strategy. The SSP must be comprehensive, accurate, and kept current. It serves as the primary document for understanding system security and is reviewed by 3PAO and authorizing officials. Effective SSP development requires understanding of the system, security controls, FEDRAMP requirements, and documentation standards. The SSP should be updated when the system changes or controls are modified.
use_cases.business_value: Creates comprehensive security documentation required for FEDRAMP authorization, enabling authorization decisions and demonstrating security compliance.
example_queries: What is included in a System Security Plan?
How do we develop an SSP?
What information is needed for each control?
How do we document control inheritance?
How do we maintain the SSP?

authorization_types.description: Single federal agency sponsors and authorizes the cloud service offering.
authorization_types.detailed_knowledge: Agency Authorization is when a single federal agency sponsors and authorizes a cloud service offering for its use. The sponsoring agency acts as the authorizing official and makes the authorization decision. Key characteristics include: single agency sponsorship, agency-specific authorization, agency acts as authorizing official, authorization is specific to that agency, and other agencies may reference the authorization. The process includes: agency identifies need for cloud service, agency sponsors the service, 3PAO conducts security assessment, agency reviews authorization package, agency makes authorization decision (ATO), and agency maintains ongoing monitoring. Agency authorization is typically faster than JAB authorization but is limited to the sponsoring agency. Other agencies can reference agency authorizations through the FEDRAMP Marketplace.

authorization_types.description: Joint Authorization Board authorizes cloud service offering for government-wide use.
authorization_types.detailed_knowledge: JAB (Joint Authorization Board) Authorization is when the JAB authorizes a cloud service offering for government-wide use. The JAB consists of representatives from DHS, DOD, and GSA. Key characteristics include: JAB acts as authorizing official, authorization is for government-wide use, more rigorous process than agency authorization, typically for high-demand services, and provides highest level of authorization. The process includes: service provider applies to JAB, JAB reviews application and selects services, 3PAO conducts comprehensive security assessment, JAB reviews authorization package, JAB makes authorization decision (P-ATO for high-impact, ATO for moderate/low-impact), and JAB maintains ongoing monitoring. JAB authorization is more rigorous and time-consuming but provides government-wide authorization. JAB-authorized services are listed in the FEDRAMP Marketplace.

common_control_areas.description: Implementing and maintaining NIST 800-53 security controls based on impact level and control baselines.
common_control_areas.detailed_knowledge: Security Control Implementation involves implementing NIST 800-53 security controls required for FEDRAMP compliance. Controls are organized into control families and have baselines for Low, Moderate, and High impact levels. Key components include: understanding control requirements from NIST 800-53, determining impact level (Low, Moderate, High), selecting appropriate control baseline, implementing controls according to baseline, documenting control implementation in SSP, testing control effectiveness, maintaining controls, addressing control deficiencies, managing control inheritance, and updating control documentation. Control implementation can be system-specific, inherited from underlying infrastructure, or provided by third parties. Effective control implementation ensures security and FEDRAMP compliance.
key_questions: What NIST 800-53 controls must be implemented?
What is the control baseline for our impact level?
How do we implement controls?
What controls can be inherited?
How do we test control effectiveness?

common_control_areas.description: Implementing ongoing security monitoring to maintain FEDRAMP authorization and demonstrate ongoing security.
common_control_areas.detailed_knowledge: Continuous Monitoring is essential for maintaining FEDRAMP authorization. It involves ongoing security monitoring, assessment, and reporting. Key components include: developing continuous monitoring strategy, implementing monitoring tools and processes, collecting security metrics (vulnerability scans, security events, configuration changes, access logs), analyzing security data, identifying security issues, reporting security status to authorizing officials, managing POA&M items, coordinating annual assessments, and maintaining continuous monitoring documentation. Continuous monitoring demonstrates that security controls remain effective and that the system continues to meet security requirements. Effective continuous monitoring enables early detection of security issues, timely remediation, and ongoing authorization maintenance.
key_questions: What are FEDRAMP continuous monitoring requirements?
What security metrics should we monitor?
How do we implement continuous monitoring?
What is required for annual assessments?
How do we report security status?

domain_knowledge_documents.content: The FEDRAMP authorization package is a comprehensive set of documents submitted to authorizing officials for authorization decisions. Key components include: System Security Plan (SSP) - comprehensive document describing the system, security controls, and control implementation; Security Assessment Report (SAR) - 3PAO's independent assessment of security controls, including test results and findings; Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) - plan for addressing security control deficiencies and weaknesses; Continuous Monitoring Strategy - strategy for ongoing security monitoring and assessment. The authorization package must be complete, accurate, and current. It is reviewed by authorizing officials who make authorization decisions (ATO, P-ATO, or denial). Effective authorization packages demonstrate comprehensive security, address all requirements, and enable positive authorization decisions.

NIST 800-53 provides security control baselines for Low, Moderate, and High impact levels. Control baselines specify which controls are required for each impact level. Low Impact baseline includes fewer controls, Moderate Impact includes more comprehensive controls, and High Impact includes the most comprehensive controls. Control baselines are organized by control families (AC, AU, CA, CM, etc.). Organizations must implement all controls specified in their baseline, document implementation in the SSP, and test control effectiveness. Controls can be system-specific, inherited from underlying infrastructure, or provided by third parties. Control inheritance must be documented. Understanding control baselines is essential for FEDRAMP compliance and effective control implementation.
